title: Chapter 1
html:
Foreword

The diaries of Samuel Pepys, when considered in the broad arc of Western letters, reveal themselves less as the mere chronicles of a seventeenth-century naval administrator than as one of the supreme monuments of the autobiographical imagination. They are the testament not of a philosopher or poet in the conventional sense, but of a civil servant who nevertheless, by force of candid utterance and minute fidelity, joins the company of Montaigne and Augustine, of Rousseau and Proust, in the endless catalog of those who have turned the examination of the self into an art equal to, if not surpassing, the grand designs of epic or tragedy. Pepys, in the apparent mundanity of recording meals, ailments, quarrels, intrigues, and amorous trespasses, bequeaths to us a textual cathedral: unpretentious in form, yet inexhaustible in substance, a book in which the centuries have discovered again and again not just England, but human consciousness in its full simultaneity of pettiness and grandeur.

To situate Pepys requires a return to the crucible of Restoration England, when monarchy, Parliament, and religion collided in unstable harmonies and dissonances. The plague, the fire, the wars with the Dutch, and the turbulent resurgence of courtly excess after the gray austerities of Cromwell—all provide the backdrop against which Pepys inscribes his testament. Yet to reduce the diary to a documentary is to miss its canon-shaping quality. Pepys is not valuable merely because he recorded what others neglected; he is great because in recording, he gave form to a style of life-writing that remains unchallenged. His frankness belongs to an age that was reinventing itself after regicide, where skepticism, theatricality, and appetites both physical and spiritual flourished anew. Pepys’s diary is at once an inward theater and a national chronicle, an act of writing in which the personal and political are never disentangled, because human beings, as Montaigne long before observed, are themselves political creatures.

What makes Pepys uniquely canonical is his refusal to filter his desires, his jealousies, his lusts, and his moral anxieties. Here is not the confessional piety of Augustine, always seeking God’s absolution, nor the carefully constructed self-portrait of Rousseau, performing virtue even in sin. Pepys is unembarrassedly human: he records the blush and the shame, but does not suppress them. His text insists that the self must be preserved entire, in contradiction as much as in coherence. In this he anticipates Proust, who would later locate art in the retrieval of sensations, however fleeting or compromising. Pepys did not seek beauty; yet beauty found him, in the texture of daily speech, in the cadences of his shorthand, in the rhythm of his worldly engagements. The greatness of the diary lies in its fidelity to life not as it ought to be lived but as it is lived, moment by moment, contradiction by contradiction.

The stylistic genius of Pepys rests in his remarkable economy of observation. His sentences, clipped yet vigorous, carry an energy akin to the prose of Shakespeare’s contemporaries, though they do not strive for poetry. The immediacy is the poetry. What he writes today, he wrote for no audience but himself; yet that very privacy bestows on us the authenticity which literary artifice too often distorts. Pepys makes no claim to rhetorical grandeur, yet his language pulses with vitality, because it was not designed to persuade but to remember. Thus it is that his diary, written for the eye alone, becomes a universal mirror. In a paradox as profound as any in literature, the least intended art becomes immortal art, the least ambitious project becomes one of the canon’s indelible monuments.

When one weighs Pepys against the longer tradition of Western self-writing, the connections blaze forth. Montaigne sought to “paint himself whole,” not as an example but as an essence of humanity. Augustine confessed his sins to reveal the restlessness of the heart until it rests in God. Rousseau exposed his intimate contradictions to claim authenticity against civilization. Pepys, by contrast, never theorized the act. He wrote, as it were, innocently—innocent not of corruption, but of literary self-consciousness. And yet the result is no less literary. Indeed, it may be the highest example of life transfigured by text without intention. His diary is the antithesis of artifice, and yet it is an art greater than artifice, because it persuades us of its absolute fidelity. Where Augustine sought salvation, Pepys seeks remembrance; where Rousseau sought justification, Pepys seeks only the record. Yet the record becomes justification in itself: the justification of living.

The historical reception of the diary was delayed, for Pepys wrote in cipher, and the manuscript lay dormant until its eventual unsealing in the nineteenth century. The Victorian rediscovery came at a moment when England, surfeited with its own imperial self-confidence, was eager to rediscover its Restoration ancestor, a man as concerned with his digestion and mistresses as with naval reform. That the diary scandalized as much as it delighted was inevitable; prudery is always unsettled by candor. But Pepys has endured not because he titillates, but because he reveals the permanent truth: that no civilization, however triumphant, escapes the frailties and appetites of the individual. He has become a touchstone for historians of culture precisely because he is inexhaustible for readers of literature. History provides the context, but literature provides the endurance.

Pepys thus stands in relation to the Western canon as a necessary corrective. Too often we exalt what is heroic, tragic, or sublime. Pepys insists on the quotidian, on the dinner, the quarrel, the market, the bedchamber. In him, literature finds its ballast, its reminder that the canon must not only contain the voices of prophets and philosophers but also those who remind us of the irreducible texture of the everyday. In that sense, Pepys is closer to Homer than he might at first appear: for the Odyssey is filled with eating, sleeping, bathing, and wandering, and it is precisely these ordinary rhythms that give grandeur to the epic. Likewise, Pepys’s diary is an epic of the ordinary, a Homeric catalogue of lived moments. What we find in him is not the elevation of life into art but the discovery that life itself, when preserved in its totality, is already art.

Philosophically, Pepys may be regarded as an unwitting precursor of existentialism. He records without offering moral resolution, presenting life as flux, as a series of states and contradictions that never resolve into a single truth. Kierkegaard and Nietzsche would later wrestle with the anxiety of existence; Pepys, untheorized, simply presents it. The diary is the phenomenology of the everyday: being and time not as abstractions but as dinners, debts, lusts, sermons, and weather. In Pepys we encounter a consciousness that does not attempt to transcend the world but is wholly immersed in it. The greatness lies in the immersion. He is the anti-Platonic writer par excellence: where Plato would ascend to the realm of forms, Pepys insists on the forms of bread and wine, of silk and candlelight. Yet in these forms he achieves a universality as profound as any philosopher’s abstraction.

The endurance of Pepys arises also from his capacity to dramatize the tension between public duty and private desire. He is, in this sense, Shakespearean. The kings and courtiers of Shakespeare are constantly torn between the weight of office and the pull of passion; Pepys, though no king, inhabits a stage equally fraught. His naval administration intersects with his pursuit of pleasure, and the diary reveals the ceaseless oscillation between the two. Like Falstaff, he relishes appetite; like Hamlet, he reflects on mortality. The diary becomes a play without spectators, a tragedy without climax, a comedy without resolution. It is all genres at once, and thus it transcends genre.

One must also observe the paradox of Pepys’s self-preservation. He recorded his intimacies in cipher, as though to protect them from prying eyes, and yet he preserved the manuscripts, as though to ensure they would be read. The contradiction is the man himself: ashamed yet proud, private yet public, fleeting yet permanent. In that paradox lies the essence of literature. For every writer of greatness is caught between concealment and revelation, between the wish to speak and the fear of being overheard. Pepys embodies this paradox so completely that he becomes its exemplar. The diary is the performance of privacy that becomes public; it is the soliloquy that becomes a chorus.

If one seeks to measure Pepys against the most enduring writers of the Western tradition, it is instructive to place him beside Dante. Dante transforms his personal political exile into the cosmic architecture of the Divine Comedy. Pepys transforms his professional career and personal indulgences into a mirror of Restoration England. Dante ascends through Hell, Purgatory, and Heaven; Pepys meanders through taverns, theaters, and bedchambers. Yet both achieve immortality, for both render their own age eternal. Where Dante reveals man in relation to God, Pepys reveals man in relation to himself. Both are indispensable, for the canon must contain both the vertical and the horizontal, both the ascent to the divine and the immersion in the human.

The aesthetic significance of Pepys lies in the way his diary dissolves the distinction between literature and life. It is written without aesthetic intention, yet it becomes a masterwork of aesthetic value. This inversion challenges our conventional categories. We call Homer and Virgil poets because they intended poetry; we call Pepys a diarist, yet he is perhaps more poetical than many poets, for he allows the pulse of life itself to shape his text. He is the supreme example of what Bloom himself might call the “strong misreading” of life: the ability to transform accident into destiny, the fleeting into the permanent. Pepys does not seek to create literature, and therefore his literature transcends creation. It is literature by default, literature as residue, yet more enduring than most deliberate works.

If we ask why Pepys remains central to the canon, the answer is that he exemplifies the truth that literature is not simply about what is written but about what is preserved. The diary survived centuries, hidden yet intact, until the nineteenth century revealed it. Its survival is itself a literary destiny, as though the text awaited its readers across ages. In that survival, Pepys resembles Kafka, whose unpublished manuscripts were preserved against his own dying wish. Literature is always partly accident, partly providence. Pepys’s diary proves that the accidents of preservation can create a canon as surely as aesthetic ambition can.

In our own era, Pepys’s relevance only deepens. We live in a world of diaries without end—digital confessions, social media feeds, unending chronicles of self. Yet what distinguishes Pepys from the glut of modern self-writing is not merely the historical context but the quality of consciousness. Pepys is not a narcissist; he is a participant-observer, engaged with the world as fully as with himself. He records not only his own hunger but the hunger of a nation. In the diary, the self is never severed from the larger social and political body. Modernity has produced a fragmentation of the self; Pepys reminds us of its unity. His diary is a whole life, and therefore a whole art.

The canon, if it means anything, must preserve those works that teach us what it means to be human. Pepys teaches us through candor, through fidelity, through the refusal to idealize. He stands with Montaigne as one of the supreme humanists, though he never theorized his humanism. His art is unconscious, and therefore pure. The Diary is not a performance for readers, but the truest record of a man, and thus, paradoxically, one of the greatest performances in literature. Pepys’s endurance is inevitable because he speaks not to one age but to all ages, not to one class but to all humanity, not to one nation but to the condition of being alive.

To read Pepys is to confront ourselves in all our banality and all our sublimity. He reminds us that literature need not transcend life to be great; it need only preserve life as it is. The grandeur of Homer, the vision of Dante, the irony of Shakespeare, the candor of Montaigne—all find their quiet echo in Pepys. His diary is the Western canon’s hidden jewel: unpolished, unpretentious, yet dazzling in its inexhaustible facets. That it continues to draw readers centuries later is proof of its inexorable claim to greatness. And in this claim, Pepys has achieved what few writers have achieved: he has turned the texture of life itself into the stuff of eternity.
